name: Buddy Morrow
abstract: Buddy Morrow (born Muni Zudekoff; February 8, 1919 – September 27, 2010), also known as Moe Zudekoff, was an American trombonist and bandleader.

about_quotes.speaker: Ken Peplowski
about_quotes.evidence: I got asked to join the Dorsey band that night by the leader, Buddy Morrow... I was on that band for about two and a half years.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ken Peplowski interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 10, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Hal Espinosa
about_quotes.evidence: a tenor sax player called — I think his name was Bob McCoy. And he was a contractor for Buddy Morrow’s band. And Buddy was looking for a lead player and they were going to offer me $175 a week if I would take it. And I thought $175, I’m making I think it was $150 then. So I took it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hal Espinosa interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, March 20, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Stanley Kay
about_quotes.evidence: I subbed in Buddy Morrow’s band. And I took the gig... So I played for three days.
about_quotes.doc_title: Stanley Kay interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 18, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Wayne Andre
about_quotes.evidence: I did a concert with Tony Bennett at Carnegie Hall. And on the first half of the concert was Robert Farnum... Buddy Morrow was actually the first trombone player... Buddy says, 'Man, why don't you play this thing?'
about_quotes.doc_title: Wayne Andre interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 7, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: I call him the iron man because Buddy Morrow used to play -- he had a band and he used to play the parts and the solos and never give out.

about_quotes.speaker: Artie Shaw
about_quotes.evidence: We went along with that band for a while... Buddy Morrow... He was immediately a talent... I had to train guys... I heard him trying.
about_quotes.doc_title: Artie Shaw — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
